metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that when `include_retired=True`, `*.retired` filenames must also be included in the `dir_hash` computation (or that the cache must be bypassed entirely for rebuild calls).
findings.finding: The plan adds `include_retired: bool = False` to `prepare_event_files`, which today computes `dir_hash` over `*.json` filenames only (line 97 of _cache.py). When called with `include_retired=True`, the function would replay `*.retired` files but the hash would still be computed without them, so the cache lookup would falsely hit on a stale pre-rebuild cache and return the old (pre-repair) compiled_state. The plan does not address this cache-coherence gap.
findings.location: Scope — reducer/_cache.py; Concrete Rules — REBUILD; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `include_retired` spec: 'When include_retired=True, the dir_hash must incorporate *.retired filenames so the cache is not falsely hit; alternatively, always bypass the cache when include_retired=True by passing a sentinel dir_hash.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify A1 (tricolour-head-ratfish) is fully merged and `RETIRED_SUFFIX`, `is_active_event`, and the `.retired` rename in `compact.py` are present in the base branch before RC2b implementation begins; add a hard merge-order gate (e.g., `assert RETIRED_SUFFIX` import guard or CI step) so the RC2b build fails fast if A1 is absent.
findings.finding: The plan treats A1's deliverables (`RETIRED_SUFFIX`, `is_active_event`, and the `.retired` rename mechanism in `compact.py`) as already present in the codebase ('depends_on, wired'), but a full-codebase Grep finds zero occurrences of either symbol or any `.retired`-suffixed event file logic in `compact.py`. Currently `compact.py` hard-deletes folded events via `os.remove` (lines 140-144) with no `.retired` rename. If A1 is not yet merged when RC2b is implemented, the `include_retired: bool` parameter on `reduce_ticket`/`prepare_event_files` will have no `.retired` files to include, the orphan-rebuild path silently does nothing, and the regression test (AC5) cannot go GREEN.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'A1 = tricolour-head-ratfish delivers the `.retired` rename + `RETIRED_SUFFIX`/`is_active_event` (depends_on, wired)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit merge-order assertion: in `_commands/fsck.py`'s orphan-rebuild path, import `RETIRED_SUFFIX` from the A1-delivered module at the top of the file (not inside a function) so a missing A1 causes an `ImportError` at load time rather than a silent no-op at runtime. Also add an AC: 'A1 merge SHA is reachable from the RC2b branch HEAD before this plan is marked complete.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm A1 is already merged (show a commit SHA or passing import) or add an explicit 'blocked until A1 merges' note and remove 'wired' language so implementors do not assume the symbols are importable.
findings.finding: The plan relies on `RETIRED_SUFFIX` and `is_active_event` from dependency A1 ('depends_on, wired') for both the `include_retired` rebuild path and the compaction horizon guard's file-listing logic, but a full codebase search confirms neither symbol exists anywhere in the source tree. 'Wired' implies these are already merged and importable, but they are not — making every code path in this plan that references them (reducer/_cache.py include_retired glob, compact.py horizon guard, fsck.py orphan scan of *.retired) unimplementable until A1 lands.
findings.location: Dependencies section — A1; Scope section — reducer/_cache.py, _commands/compact.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the Scope section that `prepare_event_files` must add a `*.retired` file collection path (separate from the `*.json` glob) when `include_retired=True`, and confirm the plan specifies this extension to the glob/filter rather than only adding a boolean parameter.
findings.finding: `prepare_event_files` uses a `*.json` glob (line 102) and a `.endswith('.json')` filter (line 97) — `.retired` files by design do NOT end in `.json`. Adding `include_retired: bool = False` to `prepare_event_files` without also changing both the file-listing filter and the glob pattern means the flag can never actually return retired files, making the rebuild path silently a no-op.
findings.location: Scope / REBUILD section; reducer/_cache.py lines 97 and 102

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `compute_dir_hash` to the Scope edit-target list and specify how the hash computation changes when `include_retired=True` (e.g., also stat `*.retired` files as hash inputs).
findings.finding: `compute_dir_hash` in `_cache.py` computes its hash solely from the `event_filenames` list passed to it (line 97 filter, `.json` only). When `include_retired=True` is used for rebuild, retired files must also be part of the hash — otherwise the cache would not invalidate when a `.retired` file changes, causing a stale rebuild. The plan does not enumerate `compute_dir_hash` as an edit target.
findings.location: Scope section; reducer/_cache.py:50 (compute_dir_hash)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item and scope note that `compute_dir_hash` in `reducer/_cache.py` must be extended to include `.retired` file names/sizes/mtimes when `include_retired=True` is in effect (or unconditionally, to future-proof), so the cache is invalidated when a `.retired` file is added or removed.
findings.finding: The `include_retired=True` path in `prepare_event_files` will produce stale cache hits unless `compute_dir_hash` is also extended to include `.retired` file metadata: the current `compute_dir_hash` (line 97 of `_cache.py`) hashes only `.json` filenames, so adding a `.retired` file (e.g., when A1 delivers its rename) will not invalidate the cache, and the rebuild will replay a stale state.
findings.location: Scope / AC line 3: 'reduce_ticket/prepare_event_files gain include_retired; default False preserves current behavior; True includes *.retired in replay' / `reducer/_cache.py` line 97
findings.suggested_fix: In `prepare_event_files`, when `include_retired=True`, extend `event_filenames` to also include `*.retired` files before passing to `compute_dir_hash`. Alternatively, always fold `.retired` files into the hash (they are excluded from normal replay by the glob filter, so the cache stays correct for `include_retired=False` — the hash just tracks more files).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` to `_SECTIONS['compact']` in `_config_schema.py` (with an `_as_int(v, k, minimum=1)` coercer) AND register a `REBAR_COMPACT_HORIZON_NS` env-var alias in `config.py`'s alias table, following the `REBAR_COMPACT_THRESHOLD` pattern; add `config.py` and `docs/config.md` to the Scope edit targets.
findings.finding: The plan adds `COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` to `CompactConfig` but does not enumerate `config.py` (the env-var alias registry) as an edit target. The established codebase pattern — enforced for the existing `compact.threshold` key — requires every `CompactConfig` field to register an env-var override alias (e.g. `REBAR_COMPACT_THRESHOLD` for `threshold`). Omitting this creates a config-surface inconsistency: operators cannot override the horizon via environment variable, breaking the same operator contract the rest of `CompactConfig` provides.
findings.location: AC line: '`COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` added to `CompactConfig` (default 1_800_000_000_000 ns)'; Scope section (does not list `config.py`)
findings.suggested_fix: In `_config_schema.py` add `'COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS': lambda v, k: _as_int(v, k, minimum=1)` to `_SECTIONS['compact']`. In `config.py` add `'REBAR_COMPACT_HORIZON_NS': ('compact', 'COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS', None)` to the env-alias table (or follow the exact existing tuple shape). Update `docs/config.md` with the new key and its env override.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable location for the rebuild counter (e.g., a `rebuild_count` key in fsck's JSON output or a `WARN rebuild_count=N` log line) and add a pytest invocation that asserts each of the four sub-claims in AC4 (tmp+rename, .bak deletion, counter, WARN).
findings.finding: AC4 bundles four distinct verifiable sub-claims (atomic tmp+rename, .bak deleted after clean round-trip, rebuild counter incremented, WARN logged) but provides no proving command for any of them, and 'rebuild counter incremented' has no specified observable location (log line, structured field, CLI output) making it unmeasurable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (fsck orphan detect + rebuild)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Rebuild counter is emitted as `rebuild_count=<N>` in a WARN log line and returned in fsck's structured output. Proving command: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/reducer/ -k fsck_orphan_rebuild -q`; fixture verifies .bak absent after run, counter==1, and WARN present in caplog.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet (and ADR section) specifying what fsck/subsequent runs do when a `.bak` file is found with no corresponding in-progress rebuild — e.g., treat as safe-to-delete after verifying the active snapshot replays cleanly, or surface a WARN.
findings.finding: The `.bak` file artifact lifecycle is only partially specified: the plan says it is 'deleted by that same process after a clean replay round-trip in the same run,' but no behavior is defined for orphaned `.bak` files that survive a mid-run crash or process kill. Subsequent fsck or operator runs encountering a stale `.bak` have no documented recovery contract, and neither the AC nor the ADR requirement calls this out.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD section) / Acceptance Criteria (fsck AC bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'If a <snap>.bak is found at fsck startup with no active rebuild in progress, verify the current snapshot replays cleanly; if so, delete the .bak and log INFO; if not, treat .bak as the recovery source and re-promote it.' Document this contract in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a cross-run `.bak` retirement path: either fsck on a subsequent run detects a stale `.bak` (with a valid current snapshot) and removes it, or the plan explicitly bounds `.bak` lifetime (e.g., delete at fsck startup if a healthy snapshot already exists for that ticket).
findings.finding: `.bak` files written during rebuild have no specified cleanup/retirement path across process runs. The plan only deletes `.bak` 'after a clean round-trip in the same run,' meaning any crash or early exit after the rename but before deletion leaves `.bak` files that accumulate indefinitely with no subsequent fsck logic to handle them.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD section): 'the old snapshot is moved to <snap>.bak by the rebuild process and deleted by that same process after a clean replay round-trip in the same run'
findings.suggested_fix: At the start of each fsck run, for any ticket where `<snap>.bak` exists alongside an intact `<snap>` (verifiable via a quick replay), delete the `.bak` immediately — this makes the cleanup idempotent and cross-run safe without requiring a lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scan_for_latest_snapshot` to the Scope edit-target list (or explicitly document that it handles retired files correctly as-is) since it receives the same `event_files` list that `include_retired=True` extends.
findings.finding: `scan_for_latest_snapshot` in `_processors.py` is called by `replay_events` with the `event_files` list. It is an independent pass over the list looking for SNAPSHOT events. If `include_retired=True` flows through `prepare_event_files` to include retired files in `event_files`, `scan_for_latest_snapshot` must also correctly handle (or skip) retired SNAPSHOT files during its scan. The plan lists `_processors.py` only for 'process_snapshot + positional skip ~648-702' and does not enumerate `scan_for_latest_snapshot` as needing changes.
findings.location: Scope section; reducer/_processors.py:624 (scan_for_latest_snapshot)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen the rebuild concurrency spec: (a) define 'quiesced' as 'holding the store write lock (`lock.acquire`) for the duration of the detect→write→verify sequence'; (b) cap the re-check/redo loop (e.g., max 2 retries, then WARN and leave the orphan for the next fsck run); (c) gate the rebuild on `no_mutate=False` (read-only fsck surfaces WARN but do not rebuild).
findings.finding: The concurrency spec for the rebuild path ('rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed') is underspecified in three ways: 'quiesced' is not defined relative to the existing write lock, the 're-check/redo' branch has no loop-termination bound, and there is no statement about whether fsck must be run in a mode that asserts `no_mutate=False` for the rebuild to proceed.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD): 'Concurrency: rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-rollback schema state: define what fsck does when it finds an existing `<snap>.bak` on a subsequent run (e.g., treat it as a rebuild-resume signal, complete the pending tmp→snap rename, or emit a WARN and re-attempt the full rebuild), and add a DATA LOSS RISK note covering the window between moving the old snapshot to .bak and committing the new one.
findings.finding: The snapshot rebuild is a two-step file-system sequence (move old snap → .bak, then rename .tmp → snap) that is NOT atomic; if the process crashes between those two steps, the ticket directory is left with a .bak but no active SNAPSHOT file. The plan does not specify what fsck does when it re-encounters a ticket already carrying a .bak, nor does it assert the expected schema state after such a partial failure — leaving the ticket in an undefined intermediate state with no documented recovery path.
findings.location: REBUILD section: 'Rebuild writes <snap>.tmp + atomic os.rename; the old snapshot is moved to <snap>.bak by the rebuild process and deleted by that same process after a clean replay round-trip in the same run.'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the rebuild sequence to: (1) write <snap>.tmp atomically, (2) atomically os.replace(.tmp, snap) — overwriting the old snapshot in one syscall (no .bak step). If rollback visibility is needed, write the .bak BEFORE step 1 and delete it only after step 2 succeeds. Document that on re-entry, a found .bak with a missing snap means step 2 did not complete: re-run os.replace(.tmp, snap) if .tmp still exists, else restore from .bak.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to each of AC1–AC3 and AC7 (e.g., `pytest tests/ -k compact_horizon -q` for AC2; `grep -r 'COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS' docs/adr/` for AC7; `pytest tests/ -k include_retired -q` for AC3).
findings.finding: ACs 1, 2, 3, and 7 each assert a behavioral or structural outcome but supply no concrete proving command (pytest path, grep invocation, or file check) to produce evidence of completion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (CompactConfig), AC2 (unit test), AC3 (include_retired), AC7 (ADR)
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/reducer/ -k compact_horizon -q`; AC3: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/reducer/ -k include_retired -q`; AC7: `grep -rE 'COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS.*1800' docs/adr/`; AC1: `python -c "from _config_schema import CompactConfig; assert CompactConfig().COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS == 1_800_000_000_000"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rebuild counter's exact placement (e.g. a `rebuild_count: int` field in the fsck return dict / a `stats` dict returned by the repair function / a module-level `RebuildsResult` namedtuple) so AC4 is verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a 'rebuild counter' (Concrete Rules: 'increment a rebuild counter'; AC4: 'rebuild counter incremented') but never specifies where that counter lives: it has no module, class, variable name, or return-value slot. The fsck command currently returns a list of tagged issue strings with no numeric summary field beyond the final `issue_count` line. An executor must invent a home for this counter.
findings.location: Concrete Rules — REBUILD (Option 1); Acceptance Criteria AC4

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the snapshot-rebuild file-operation sequence as: (1) write <snap>.tmp, (2) atomic rename(<snap>.tmp, <snap>) [POSIX atomic, replaces old snap in one step], (3) move old snap (captured before step 2) to <snap>.bak — and describe the resume behaviour when a .bak is found on startup (i.e., the rollback/recovery path).
findings.finding: The snapshot rebuild sequence describes both an atomic tmp→snap rename AND a .bak safety copy, but the ordering of those two operations is ambiguous — the described sequence ('writes <snap>.tmp + atomic os.rename; the old snapshot is moved to <snap>.bak') could be read as move-old-to-.bak THEN rename-tmp-to-snap, leaving a window where neither path exists. The rollback path (.bak) is mentioned but no explicit recovery procedure for a mid-sequence crash is specified.
findings.location: Concrete Rules (REBUILD section) — snapshot atomic tmp+rename + .bak lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify the sequence as: write tmp → atomic rename(tmp, snap) [this is the single atomic step that replaces the old snapshot] → copy/rename old snap to .bak for auditability. On resume, if .bak exists and no corresponding tmp exists, treat .bak as the authoritative prior state and verify the current snap is valid; if snap is corrupt, restore from .bak.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-ticket or per-repo mutual-exclusion mechanism for the rebuild path (e.g., an advisory lock file or an atomic `os.rename(<snap> → <snap>.bak)` treated as a CAS — only the process that succeeds the rename proceeds with rebuild; the other detects the rename failure and skips or waits).
findings.finding: Concurrent fsck processes (or a fsck run overlapping with a compaction cycle) both operating on the same ticket's snapshot have no specified mutual-exclusion mechanism. Both could detect the same orphan, both attempt `os.rename(<snap> → <snap>.bak)`, and then each proceeds to write its own `.tmp` and rename it to `<snap>` — the second rename silently overwrites the first's result with no conflict detection.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD section): 'Concurrency: rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed'
findings.suggested_fix: Treat the `os.rename(<snap> → <snap>.bak)` as the CAS gate: if rename raises `FileNotFoundError` or `FileExistsError` (because `.bak` already exists), the second fsck instance skips the rebuild for that ticket and logs a debug message. This is lock-free and idempotent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define precisely what constitutes a 'clean replay round-trip' for gating `.bak` deletion (e.g., 'a second `reduce_ticket(include_retired=False)` on the same directory produces a non-error state AND `_check_snapshot` reports zero ORPHAN_EVENT/SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT findings for this ticket') and document the resumability behavior when `.bak` already exists on fsck entry.
findings.finding: The 'clean replay round-trip' criterion that gates `.bak` deletion is not defined: the plan says the old snapshot's `.bak` is deleted 'after a clean replay round-trip in the same run' but never specifies what passes or fails that round-trip check, leaving the implementer unable to write a deterministic exit condition.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD) / AC line 4: 'the old snapshot is moved to <snap>.bak ... deleted by that same process after a clean replay round-trip in the same run'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the REBUILD section: 'Round-trip criterion: after writing the new snapshot, call `reduce_ticket(ticket_dir, include_retired=False)` and verify the result has no `status` of `error`/`fsck_needed`; additionally, re-run `_check_snapshot` for the ticket and confirm zero findings. Only then delete `.bak`. If `.bak` is found on fsck entry with a valid new snapshot already present (crash-resume case), skip straight to the round-trip check and delete on pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit resumability contract for the `<snap>.bak` re-entry case: specify whether a pre-existing .bak causes fsck to skip, re-attempt, or treat as a completed rebuild, so that repeated interrupted runs converge rather than accumulate state.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what fsck's orphan-detect + rebuild logic does when it encounters a ticket directory that already has a `<snap>.bak` from a prior interrupted rebuild run — there is no stated idempotency contract for the presence of a stale .bak.
findings.location: REBUILD section (AC line: 'fsck detects a pre-snapshot orphan and rebuilds the snapshot (atomic tmp+rename; .bak deleted only after a clean round-trip in the same run; rebuild counter incremented; WARN logged)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a maximum retry count (e.g. 3 attempts) and a give-up behavior (log WARN, leave .bak in place, skip this ticket) to the quiescence-retry protocol.
findings.finding: The quiescence-retry protocol in the REBUILD spec ('rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed') has no iteration bound. If a continuous writer keeps appending events, the re-check loop never terminates. This is a fallback sub-step with an unspecified termination condition.
findings.location: Concrete Rules — REBUILD (Option 1), last sentence: 'Concurrency: rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Retry at most N=3 times; on the Nth failure log WARN "rebuild unstable after N retries — skipping" and leave the .bak file in place for manual inspection.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the fsck AC bullet) that explicitly verifies the rebuild concurrency contract: either the rebuild is gated on quiescence, or the post-rebuild event log is re-checked and the rebuild is retried if a new event landed during the rebuild.
findings.finding: The Concrete Rules describe a concurrency safety requirement ('rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed') that has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion — an implementer can skip this constraint and still pass all ACs.
findings.location: Concrete Rules (REBUILD section) vs Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 4 to read: '…; after rename, re-scan the event log for the ticket and redo the rebuild if any event arrived during the rebuild window, OR document and test that rebuild is only triggered when the store is quiesced.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC for at least one rebuild failure path: specify expected behavior (e.g., WARN log, counter increment, no data loss, idempotent retry) when rebuild cannot complete cleanly, and for the concurrency redo branch when a new event lands during rebuild.
findings.finding: The fsck orphan-detect and rebuild path has no failure-mode test specified: the plan does not require any AC covering what happens when rebuild is attempted but fails (e.g., .retired files missing/corrupt, rename fails, or a concurrent new event lands during rebuild requiring the redo branch).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'fsck detects a pre-snapshot orphan and rebuilds the snapshot...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a complementary unit-test AC asserting that an event older than the horizon (`physical_now()-event_ts >= COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS`) IS folded, so the test suite validates both sides of the horizon guard.
findings.finding: The compaction-horizon AC specifies only that a young event is NOT folded, but omits the complementary assertion that an event older than the horizon IS folded. An implementation that never folds any event would pass the stated test, leaving the feature's core function (eventual compaction of mature events) unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Unit test: an event younger than the horizon (physical_now()-event_ts < COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS) is NOT folded.'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC to: 'Unit tests: (a) an event younger than the horizon is NOT folded; (b) an event at or beyond the horizon IS folded; (c) an event exactly at the boundary (event_ts == physical_now - COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS) is folded (boundary inclusive).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the dual-write window mitigation concretely: specify that the rebuild acquires the store write lock (same lock as compact.py uses) for the tmp-write + rename sequence, OR document a bounded re-check protocol (e.g., max N retries, compare event-file list before and after rename, emit WARN if still racing after N attempts).
findings.finding: The concurrent-write dual-write window mitigation is underspecified: the plan says 'rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed', but 'quiesced' is undefined in a multi-clone git-based system (no write lock is held during fsck), and 're-check the event log' does not define what constitutes a successful check or how many retries/redos are bounded.
findings.location: REBUILD section: 'Concurrency: rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define exactly which event JSON field `event_ts` maps to in the horizon guard (e.g. `event['timestamp']`) and specify how to handle HLC ticks that exceed `hlc.physical_now()` (e.g. treat as age=0, i.e. always skip folding such events).
findings.finding: The plan's horizon-guard formula `hlc.physical_now() - event_ts >= COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` uses `event_ts` but never specifies which JSON field it maps to. In compact.py today, events carry `event.get('timestamp')` which is an HLC tick — a value that can be strictly greater than the raw wall-clock nanoseconds returned by `hlc.physical_now()` (HLC advances past physical time to maintain monotonicity). The plan asserts HLC uses `time.time_ns()` so the units match, but doesn't address the case where an HLC tick exceeds `physical_now()`, making the subtraction yield a negative value and every such event appear 'younger than the horizon'.
findings.location: Concrete Rules — SETTLED (Option 3), horizon guard formula
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bare formula with: `age_ns = max(0, hlc.physical_now() - event.get('timestamp', 0)); fold_ok = age_ns >= COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS`. This makes the field name explicit and clamps negative deltas to zero (treats future-HLC events as age=0, conservatively skipping them).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the preferred concurrency strategy explicitly (e.g., 're-check-and-redo is the default; quiescence is only applicable if a global write-lock already exists') so the executor can proceed without choosing.
findings.finding: The concurrency handling strategy is specified as 'rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed' — an OR between two meaningfully different implementation approaches (quiescence detection vs. optimistic retry loop), leaving a non-trivial design choice to the executor.
findings.location: Concrete rules — REBUILD (Option 1), concurrency paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the OR with: 'Concurrency: after writing <snap>.tmp, re-read the event log and redo if any new events appeared since the rebuild started (optimistic retry, max 3 attempts). Quiescence-based locking is out of scope for this ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'quiesced' alternative with a concrete, scoped definition (e.g., 'no writer lock is held' or 'event log unchanged since rebuild started as verified by re-check') or drop it in favor of the 're-check after rebuild' path exclusively.
findings.finding: The concurrency clause uses the undefined term 'quiesced' ('rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed') without defining what quiescence means in this system or how it is detected, introducing an underspecified coupling point between the rebuild logic and operational/runtime state.
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD section) — concurrency clause
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'rebuild only when quiesced OR re-check the event log after rebuild and redo if a new event landed' with 're-check the event log after each rebuild attempt; if new events landed, redo up to N times (default 3) before logging ERROR and deferring to next fsck run.' This eliminates the undefined quiescence concept.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for mid-rebuild failures: document that a stale `<snap>.tmp` is always overwritten (not reused) on the next rebuild attempt, and that fsck re-detects the orphan independently of `.tmp` presence (i.e., orphan detection reads the live snapshot, not the tmp file).
findings.finding: The rebuild path is described as 'idempotent + resumable' but the plan does not specify the error-handling behavior when the rebuild itself fails mid-execution (e.g., exception in `reduce_ticket`, disk-full writing `.tmp`, or crash after `.tmp` is written but before `os.rename`). It is unclear whether a subsequent run will re-detect the orphan (because the snapshot was not yet replaced) or will silently skip it (because some intermediate state exists).
findings.location: Concrete rules (REBUILD section): 'Rebuild writes <snap>.tmp + atomic os.rename; Idempotent + resumable'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC or Concrete Rules: 'If `<snap>.tmp` exists at rebuild start, it is unconditionally overwritten. Orphan detection reads only `<snap>` (never `.tmp`), ensuring a failed prior rebuild is transparently retried.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the expected bound on `|event_ts - physical_now()|` under normal HLC operation, and confirm (or adjust) the horizon guard direction and formula to account for HLC ticks advancing ahead of wall clock — add a code comment or ADR note stating the invariant the guard relies on.
findings.finding: The horizon guard `hlc.physical_now() - event_ts >= COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` is asserted as correct and safe, but HLC ticks are guaranteed to be >= physical_now() (they advance past wall clock via the `max(..., physical_now()) + 1` formula and by witnessing remote events). An event written after observing a far-future peer timestamp will have event_ts >> physical_now() for an extended period, making the effective age (physical_now() - event_ts) negative or smaller than the real wall-clock age. The plan does not verify or bound this HLC-vs-physical-clock divergence, leaving the horizon guard's precision unresolved for the adversarial case.
findings.location: Concrete rules (Option 3) / compact.py horizon fold guard
findings.suggested_fix: Add a doc-comment to the horizon guard: `# HLC ticks satisfy event_ts >= physical_now_at_write; the guard fires when wall-clock has advanced past event_ts by at least COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS, which under normal skew (<< 1800 s) is equivalent to 'event is at least 1800 s old'. Under large HLC advances (witnessing a far-future peer), the guard is conservative (holds the event longer), never aggressive.` This makes the safety reasoning explicit without changing behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the Decision section explaining why both mechanisms are needed together: Option 3 (horizon) prevents new orphans going forward, but Option 1 (rebuild) is required to heal the ~11 existing live orphans that the horizon cannot retroactively prevent, and inline fsck detection is preferred over a one-time migration because orphans may continue to appear (e.g., from pre-horizon clones) and need ongoing detection.
findings.finding: The plan's Decision section defers Option 2 (per-field LWW-by-timestamp) with no positive rationale for why Options 1+3 together are the right combination — specifically, no explanation of why the time-based horizon alone is insufficient to resolve the 11 live orphans (it isn't: the horizon only prevents new ones), and why rebuild was chosen over the alternative of a one-time migration script that re-snapshots affected tickets.
findings.location: Decision section: 'Option 2 (per-field LWW-by-timestamp) deferred as a possible follow-up'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a plan-body section describing what determinism work RC2b performs (e.g., 'sort is applied before reduce; listdir order is normalised inside prepare_event_files') so the AC maps to concrete deliverables, or (b) move the determinism test to a separate pre-existing test suite and remove it from this plan's ACs.
findings.finding: The Determinism AC ('reduce_ticket run twice on the same file set returns equal compiled_state; Proof: pytest -k determinism') is an orphan criterion: no described work in the plan body adds or changes anything to achieve or guarantee determinism in this change, so there is no deliverable for the AC to verify.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — determinism bullet vs Concrete Rules / Scope sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `include_retired=True` needs to be a parameter on the existing public `reduce_ticket`/`prepare_event_files` API (40+ call-sites, only 2 True-callers named), or whether a dedicated `rebuild_snapshot_from_log(ticket_dir)` helper would isolate the *.retired-reading logic without widening the public API.
findings.finding: `include_retired: bool = False` is proposed as a new parameter on `reduce_ticket` and `prepare_event_files`, both of which are public API with 40+ existing call-sites, but the plan names only 2 anticipated callers that would ever pass `True` (the fsck rebuild path in this plan and A3). This falls below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a new branching parameter on a widely-used API.
findings.location: Concrete rules section: 'This requires a NEW `include_retired: bool = False` parameter on `reduce_ticket` (`reducer/_api.py`) AND on `prepare_event_files` (`reducer/_cache.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a private `_rebuild_from_full_log(ticket_dir)` function in `reducer/_api.py` that constructs its own sorted file list including `*.retired` and calls `replay_events` directly, rather than adding `include_retired` to `reduce_ticket`/`prepare_event_files`. This keeps the public API stable and avoids the dir_hash cache-coherence question.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
